name: Red Wine
cultural_notes: Widely produced around the world with countless grape varieties and styles.
safety_notes: Contains tannins and histamines which can trigger headaches in sensitive individuals
Usually higher ABV than white wine
recommendations: Drink one glass of water for every glass of wine
Pair with protein (like meat or cheese) to soften the impact of tannins

name: White Wine
cultural_notes: Often served chilled, popular in warmer months and with lighter fare.
safety_notes: High acidity can cause heartburn or acid reflux
Often easier to drink quickly due to chilled temperature
recommendations: Sip slowly to appreciate the aromatics
Avoid drinking on an empty stomach due to high acidity

name: Sparkling Wine / Champagne
cultural_notes: Champagne is specifically from the Champagne region of France. Cava and Prosecco are popular regional alternatives.
safety_notes: Carbonation causes alcohol to be absorbed into the bloodstream faster
Pressure in the bottle requires careful opening
recommendations: Limit consumption during toasts to avoid a 'spike' in blood alcohol
Always keep the bottle pointed away from people when opening

name: Malbec
cultural_notes: Argentina's signature grape. Thrives at high altitudes in Mendoza.
safety_notes: Generally higher ABV (14%+) than many European reds
recommendations: Pair with hearty foods to balance the higher alcohol content

name: Carmenere
cultural_notes: Chile's signature grape, rediscovered in 1994. Often mistaken for Merlot.
safety_notes: Standard red wine risks; generally moderate tannins
recommendations: Excellent with spiced or peppery dishes

name: Ice Wine
cultural_notes: Made from grapes frozen on the vine. Canada and Germany are the largest producers.
safety_notes: Extremely high sugar content can cause a 'sugar crash' or headache
Small serving sizes (2oz) naturally limit alcohol intake
recommendations: Serve in very small quantities as a digestif
Brush teeth after consumption due to high sugar and acidity

name: Prosecco
cultural_notes: Italy's most popular sparkling wine. Made using the Charmat (tank) method.
safety_notes: Lower ABV than Champagne, but still features rapid absorption from bubbles
risk_factors: Easy to over-consume during long brunches
recommendations: Great for 'Spritz' cocktails to lower the ABV even further

name: Port Wine
cultural_notes: A fortified wine from the Douro Valley. Fortified with brandy during fermentation.
safety_notes: Significantly higher ABV (20%) than standard wine
High sugar content can lead to severe hangovers
risk_factors: Rapid intoxication if poured in standard wine portions
recommendations: Treat more like a spirit than a wine

name: Tej
cultural_notes: Ethiopian honey wine flavored with gesho leaves. Served in a 'berele' flask.
safety_notes: Homemade versions can vary in ABV (up to 11%)
Honey base makes it very smooth and easy to drink
recommendations: Eat with Injera and doro wat to balance the sweetness and slow absorption

name: Chillable Reds (Grenache, Pinot Noir)
cultural_notes: Lighter red wines meant to be served slightly chilled.
safety_notes: Chilled temperature can mask the taste of alcohol, leading to faster consumption
risk_factors: Faster pace of drinking compared to room-temperature reds
recommendations: Keep the bottle in a bucket of ice to maintain the refreshing quality, but track your glass count

name: Pomegranate Wine (Azerbaijan)
cultural_notes: Azerbaijan is famous for its pomegranates. This wine is a cultural specialty.
safety_notes: High antioxidant content but also high natural acidity
recommendations: Serve chilled with savory Azerbaijani snacks like cheese or nuts
